sources: ISSN Position Stand: Nutrient Timing (JISSN, 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0189-4
ACSM Position Stand: Nutrition and Athletic Performance (Med Sci Sports Exerc, 2016)
ISSN Position Stand: Protein and Exercise (JISSN, 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0177-8
note: General population guidelines — not personal medical or clinical nutrition advice.

pre_workout.purpose: Top up glycogen stores, prime muscle protein synthesis, prevent muscle breakdown.

timing_windows.example_meal: Oats with banana and a small serve of Greek yogurt (lactose-free) or tofu scramble on wholegrain toast
Banana with a small handful of almonds, or oatmeal with a boiled egg (if not vegetarian)

pre_workout.vegetarian_notes: For vegetarians: combine legumes + grains (e.g. rice and dal) for complete amino acid profile pre-workout. Plant-based protein digests slightly slower — eat the larger meal 2–3 hrs before, not 30 min.

post_workout.purpose: Replenish glycogen, stimulate muscle protein synthesis (MPS), reduce muscle breakdown.
post_workout.timing: Within 2 hours of training. The 30-minute 'anabolic window' is real but less critical if total daily protein is adequate (ISSN 2017).
post_workout.leucine_note: 700–3000 mg leucine per serving drives MPS. Found in: Greek yogurt, eggs, legumes+rice, soy.
example_meals: Greek yogurt (lactose-free) with banana and oats — protein + fast carbs, ready in 2 min
Chickpea and spinach bowl over brown rice — complete protein when combined
Quinoa with roasted vegetables and tahini — all three macros, 20+ g protein

strength_hypertrophy.timing_emphasis: Post-workout protein within 2 hours is important

endurance_cardio.carbs_g_per_kg_per_day: 6–10 on heavy days, 3–5 on rest days
endurance_cardio.timing_emphasis: Pre-workout carbs critical; intra-workout carbs for sessions > 60 min

hiit.primary_goal: Both glycogen and muscle — combined demands

yoga_mobility.timing_emphasis: General meal quality more important than precise timing

bulking.goal: Maximise muscle gain with minimal fat gain (lean bulk)
bulking.caloric_surplus_kcal: 200–300 above maintenance (aggressive bulk risks excess fat gain)
bulking.carbs_g_per_kg: 5–7 on training days, 3–4 on rest days
bulking.training_day_focus: Higher carbs pre and post workout to fuel performance and replenish glycogen
bulking.vegetarian_note: Harder to hit caloric surplus on plants — add calorie-dense options: avocado, nut butters (not peanut if allergy), seeds, olive oil, full-fat yogurt (LF).

cutting.goal: Lose fat while preserving lean muscle
cutting.carbs_g_per_kg: 2–4 (reduce from training day amounts, keep around workouts)
cutting.training_day_focus: Keep carbs around the workout window; cut carbs on rest days first
cutting.satiety_strategy: High-volume low-calorie foods: non-starchy veg, legumes, Greek yogurt (LF)
cutting.vegetarian_note: Protein preservation on a cut is harder on plants — prioritise legumes and tofu at every meal.

maintenance.goal: Hold body composition while maintaining performance
maintenance.note: Most people benefit from spending the majority of their time here — cycling bulk/cut continuously increases fat storage risk.

meal_timing_principles: Distribute protein evenly: 20–40 g per meal, 4–5 times per day is more effective than one large dose (ISSN 2017).
Do not train fasted for hypertrophy goals — eat a protein+carb snack at minimum 30–60 min before.
For vegetarians: eating plant proteins at each meal is especially important given lower leucine density per gram.
Carb timing matters most for endurance athletes; for general fitness, total daily intake is the priority.
